# Chapter 4: Choke The Flow: Lock Down The Gates

You think your smart fridge needs to talk to a server in Beijing at 3 AM? You think your thermostat is just checking the weather when it blasts out 50,000 packets a minute? Wake up, chief. Your IoT devices are snitching on you 24/7, and right now, your network is a club with no bouncer. Any packet with a fake ID walks right in, grabs a drink, and starts a fight. We’re fixing that today. We’re choking the flow.

## Core Carnage (Rip Apart the Essentials)

You already did the recon. You segmented the trash into its own VLAN. You slapped 802.1X on the door so only verified hardware gets in. Highkey impressive, bro. But you built a vault, put a titanium door on it, and left the back window wide open. 

A closed door doesn't mean crap if the devices inside are allowed to scream your personal data to the entire globe. Zero Trust isn't just about who enters. It’s about what leaves. 

**Default-Deny: The Art of "No"**

Default-deny. Say it with me. Default. Deny. 

It means your firewall starts as a brick wall. Nothing gets out. Nothing gets in. Zero. Zilch. Nada. Most lazy slackers set up firewalls with a default-allow rule because they don't want their Spotify to buffer. That’s highkey delusional. You build a default-deny policy, and then you carve out tiny, microscopic holes for the exact traffic that *needs* to pass. If a device doesn't have a signed permission slip? It gets dropped into the void. 

**Outbound Filtering: Leashing the Snitches**

Your smart TV literally only needs to talk to Netflix, Hulu, and maybe one update server. So why does it have a rule allowing it to talk to any IP on port 443? Because you’re lazy. 

We don't do lazy here. We do strict outbound filtering. You find the exact domains the device needs to function. You whitelist those domains. You drop everything else. If your smart lightbulb tries to ping a random AWS bucket to phone home its telemetry data? Dropped. If your robot vacuum tries to send your floorplan to a shady analytics firm? Dropped. 

**DNS Lockdown: No Rogue Queries**

DNS is the phonebook of the internet. If you don't control DNS, you don't control the network. Right now, any device on your network can just decide to use 8.8.8.8 (Google's DNS) and bypass your local resolver completely. 

Not on my watch. You force every single device to use your local DNS resolver (like Pi-hole or AdGuard Home). How? Firewall rules, baby. You block port 53 outbound for everything except your local DNS server. If a device tries to look up a domain externally, the firewall chops the connection. They use your resolver, or they don't get internet. Period.

**Geo-IP Blocking: Nuke the Foreign Rats**

Why is your baby monitor trying to establish a connection with an IP address in Eastern Europe? It shouldn't be. Geo-IP blocking takes the entire map of the internet and paints a giant red X over countries you don't trust. If your devices only need to talk to domestic servers, you block every foreign IP range at the firewall. Kill the connection attempts before they even finish the handshake. 

Here is the flow, chief. Study it. 

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[IoT Device Requests Internet] -->|Hits the Firewall| B{Default-Deny Rule}
    B -->|No Match| C[🚫 Dropped to the Void]
    B -->|Matches Whitelist| D{DNS Check}
    D -->|Port 53 to External IP| C
    D -->|Routed to Local Resolver| E{Domain Allowed?}
    E -->|No| C
    E -->|Yes| F{Geo-IP Check}
    F -->|Foreign IP| C
    F -->|Domestic/Allowed IP| G[✅ Traffic Allowed Out]
```

## Street Smarts (How to Wield This in the Wild)

Alright, dreamer. Time to get your hands dirty. You wanted zero trust? This is where the magic happens. Grab your dedicated firewall—could be a Netgate appliance running pfSense, a custom box with OPNsense, or OpenWrt if you're hustling on a budget. 

**Step 1: The Blueprint of "No"**

Log into your firewall. Go to your rules. Delete every default allow rule you see. Wipe the slate clean. Create a single "Drop All" rule at the bottom of your IoT VLAN ruleset. 

Oh, sure, skip the basics—because mediocrity's a great look on you. Don't skip this. If you don't put the drop-all rule at the bottom, nothing gets blocked. 

**Step 2: The DNS Stranglehold**

Before you open up any outbound internet, you gotta lock down the DNS. 
1. Set up a local resolver. Pi-hole is easy. AdGuard Home is meaner. Run it on your server or a Raspberry Pi.
2. Go to your firewall rules for the IoT VLAN. Create a rule that allows TCP/UDP port 53 traffic *only* to the IP address of your local resolver.
3. Create a rule right below it that blocks TCP/UDP port 53 to *any* destination. 

> ⚠️ **Common Mistake:** Forgetting to block port 53 globally after allowing it locally. If you only allow it to the resolver but don't block the rest, smart devices with hardcoded DNS (like 8.8.8.8) will just bypass your Pi-hole. You gotta choke them out.

**Step 3: Slicing the Outbound (The Whitelist Grind)**

This is where the weak quit. This takes time. This takes patience. You actually have to look at logs. 

Turn on your firewall logging for the IoT VLAN. Watch what gets dropped. Your smart TV tries to reach `api.samsung.com`. You verify that's legit. You create an alias in your firewall for `api.samsung.com` and allow port 443 outbound. 

Repeat this for every single device. 
- Smart TV? Only streaming domains and update servers.
- Thermostat? Only the manufacturer's API endpoint.
- Security cameras? Only your NVR or cloud storage bucket.

> 💡 **Pro Tip:** Use firewall aliases that support FQDNs (Fully Qualified Domain Names). IoT manufacturers change IP addresses constantly. If you whitelist IPs, you'll be playing whack-a-mole every week. Whitelist the domain, let the firewall resolve it dynamically.

**Step 4: Force DNS Over TLS (DoT) for the Resolver**

Your local resolver is now the boss, but who does *it* talk to? If it sends plain text DNS queries to upstream servers, your ISP is still snooping. Go into your Pi-hole or AdGuard settings. Set your upstream DNS to Cloudflare (1.1.1.1) or Quad9 (9.9.9.9). Enable DNS Over TLS (DoT) or DNS Over HTTPS (DoH). Now your resolver encrypts the queries. The ISP sees nothing but encrypted garbage. 

**Step 5: Geo-IP Annihilation**

Time to kill the foreign rats. 
In OPNsense or pfSense, you can install the `os-geoip` or `pfBlockerNG` package. This pulls down massive lists of IP addresses assigned to specific countries. 

Set up a block rule for your IoT VLAN. 
- Source: IoT VLAN.
- Destination: Any.
- Geo-IP: Check every box that isn't your home country. 

Boom. If a device tries to reach a server in a country you didn't whitelist, the firewall drops it like a bad habit. 

> ☕ **Real Talk:** You are going to break things. Your smart plug is going to refuse to connect. Your TV apps are going to timeout. This is normal. You are starving the spyware. When a device breaks, you check the firewall logs, see what domain it was trying to reach, decide if it's actually necessary for the device to function, and either whitelist it or leave it blocked. It's a grind, chief. Embrace the grind.

> 🎯 **Key Insight:** The goal isn't to make your network completely air-gapped. The goal is absolute visibility. When you control every domain, force local DNS, and block foreign IPs, you know exactly what is leaving your house. No surprises. No mystery telemetry. Just clean, authorized traffic.

Excuses? Cute. But winners bleed sweat, not stories. Your move. Get to the firewall and start dropping packets.

# Chapter 5: Eyes On The Streets: Sniff Out The Rats

You hear that? That’s the sound of your smart fridge quietly whispering your network secrets to a server in some godforsaken basement. While you been sitting there thinking your firewall rules make you the cyber pope of your household, your thermostat is highkey snitching to command and control. Wake up, chief! Locking the doors doesn’t mean squat if you let the rats already inside chew through the walls. It’s time to put eyes on the streets. We don’t just block traffic; we watch it, profile it, and crush it.

## Core Carnage (Rip Apart the Essentials)

You set up the choke points in Chapter 4. You built the walls. You segregated the smart TVs from the laptops. Good for you, slacker. But a wall without a guard tower is just an obstacle. In a true zero-trust crib, we don’t trust the traffic just because it passed a firewall rule. We monitor the living hell out of it.

We are hunting east-west traffic. That’s hacker speak for lateral movement. North-south is traffic going in and out of your network. East-west is the IoT camera talking to your laptop. Or the smart plug talking to the camera. If malware jumps on your fridge, it’s gonna try to move east to your laptop to find the real loot. You need to catch that sideways hustle.

**The Arsenal:**

1. **Intrusion Detection System (IDS):** Think of this as a bouncer with a photographic memory. It sits on your firewall, mirrors all the traffic, and checks it against a massive book of known gang signs. We use Suricata or Snort. They don’t block; they watch and scream when they see something dirty.
2. **DNS Sinkhole:** Your IoT devices use DNS to find the internet’s phonebook. A sinkhole poisons that phonebook. When your infected smart bulb tries to call "badserver.ru," the sinkhole says, "Yeah, that’s at 0.0.0.0." The call drops. The rat starves.
3. **Beaconing Analysis:** Malware doesn’t scream once. It whispers on a schedule. It beacons out every 30 seconds, or 60 seconds, or 2 minutes to say "I'm here, give me orders." You gotta analyze traffic patterns to spot that robotic heartbeat. Humans don’t browse like that.
4. **Automated Alerts:** If you expect to stare at logs all day, you’re delusional. You need the system to slap you in the face when the rules trip. Telegram bot, email, whatever. Just make sure it screams loud and fast.

Still with me, or you zoning out already? This is the meat, chief. Chew on it.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[IoT Device] -->|DNS Request: bad-domain.ru| B(Pi-hole / AdGuard)
    B -->|IP Match in Blocklist| C{Sinkhole}
    C -->|Returns 0.0.0.0| A
    C -->|Logs Event| D[Alert System]
    A -->|Attempts Direct IP Connection| E(OPNsense / pfSense Firewall)
    E -->|Mirrors Traffic| F(Suricata IDS)
    F -->|Detects C2 Beaconing| D
    D -->|Slaps You on Telegram| G((You, Awake & Alert))
```

## Street Smarts (How to Wield This in the Wild)

Alright, dreamer. Time to get your hands dirty. You wanted zero trust? This is where the magic happens. Grab that OPNsense or pfSense box we set up last time. We’re turning it into a surveillance state.

**Step 1: Deploy the IDS (Suricata on OPNsense)**

You need eyes on the east-west streets. We’re using Suricata because it’s mean, fast, and built into OPNsense.

1. Go to `Services -> Intrusion Detection -> Administration`.
2. Enable Suricata. Set it to run on your LAN interface (or all your VLAN interfaces). 
3. Go to `Download` and grab the rules. ET Open is free. Use it.
4. Enable the rules you want. Don’t just click all of them or you’ll melt your CPU. Focus on IoT, C2, and Trojan categories.
5. Go to `Policy` and set it to `Balanced`.
6. Hit `Apply`.

> ⚠️ **Common Mistake:** Running Suricata in Inline mode (IPS) before you tune it in Alert mode. You will block your own wife's phone from getting Instagram and ruin your weekend. Run it in Alert mode first. Watch the logs. Tune the false positives. Then flip the switch to drop.

Oh, sure, skip the basics—because mediocrity's a great look on you. Don't skip the tuning. If you don't tune, you’ll get 10,000 alerts an hour about normal Windows telemetry and you’ll miss the actual hacker walking out the back door with your data.

**Step 2: Choke the DNS with a Sinkhole**

We talked about Pi-hole or AdGuard Home in Chapter 4. Now we weaponize it.

1. Log into your Pi-hole.
2. Go to `Adlists` and add some malicious domain blocklists. Grab the OISD list or the StevenBlack list. These are massive databases of known bad neighborhoods.
3. Update gravity.
4. Now, force every device on your IoT VLAN to use this Pi-hole for DNS. You do this at the firewall level. In OPNsense, go to your DHCP settings for the IoT VLAN and set the DNS server to the Pi-hole’s IP.
5. Block port 53 outbound on your firewall for the IoT VLAN. 

> 💡 **Pro Tip:** IoT devices are sneaky. If they have hardcoded DNS (like 8.8.8.8), they will bypass your Pi-hole. You must firewall block port 53 outbound and redirect all DNS requests to your sinkhole. In OPNsense, use a NAT Port Forward rule for DNS highjacking.

**Step 3: Sniff Out the Beaconing**

This is where you separate the script kiddies from the street bosses. You got logs. Now read them.

1. Open your Pi-hole query log.
2. Look for a device making the exact same DNS request at the exact same interval. Every 30 seconds. Every 1 minute. 
3. Or, open Suricata alerts and look for ET POLICY rules. Look for repeated connections to weird IP addresses on non-standard ports.
4. If your smart plug is calling out to a random IP in Eastern Europe every 45 seconds, that’s not a software update. That’s beaconing. That’s a rat.

> 🎯 **Key Insight:** Beaconing isn't always fast. Advanced malware uses "jitter"—random delays between check-ins to avoid looking like a robot. It might call home at 30s, then 45s, then 28s. You're looking for the average interval, not a perfect metronome.

**Step 4: Set Up the Automated Slap**

You got a life, right? You ain't staring at Suricata logs on a Friday night. Actually, knowing you, slacker, maybe you do. But let's pretend you don't.

1. In OPNsense, go to `System -> Log Files -> Alerts`.
2. Set up a mailer. Use SMTP to send an email when a high-severity alert hits.
3. Better yet, use a plugin to send alerts to a Telegram bot or Discord webhook. 
4. Trigger on: Suricata high severity, Pi-hole blocking a known C2 domain, or firewall denying an outbound connection from a restricted VLAN.

> ☕ **Real Talk:** You will get alert fatigue. At first, your inbox will explode. You will want to turn it off. Don't. Spend a weekend whitelisting the noisy stuff. Apple devices are notorious for calling home constantly. Whitelist Apple telemetry if you trust it. Then, the only alerts you get are the ones that matter.

Excuses? Cute. But winners bleed sweat, not stories. Your move. You can set this up this weekend, or you can wait until your bank account gets drained because your smart toaster sold your banking session token. The tools are free. The knowledge is right here. Get to work.

## 🧠 Quick Fire Round

**Q1: Chief, why am I blocking outbound Port 53 on my IoT VLAN when I already told them to use Pi-hole for DNS?**
<details>
<summary>Reveal Answer</summary>
Because IoT manufacturers are sneaky rats, bro! Many devices have hardcoded DNS servers (like 8.8.8.8 or 1.1.1.1) baked into their firmware. If they can't reach your Pi-hole, they bypass it and call their own DNS. Blocking outbound Port 53 forces them to use your DHCP-assigned DNS, or they get no internet at all. Total chokehold.
</details>

**Q2: You spot your smart TV making a DNS request to "telemetry.samsung.com" every 60 seconds on the dot. Is this beaconing, or just a noisy device?**
<details>
<summary>Reveal Answer</summary>
It's noisy, but it fits the exact definition of beaconing! However, context is king, chief. This is likely standard telemetry (Samsung phoning home for updates or ad tracking), not malware. The trick is to recognize the pattern, identify if the domain is legit, and if you don't like it... sinkhole it. If it was calling "c2-server.ru" every 60 seconds, then you got a real rat.
</details>

# Chapter 6: Auto-Strikes: Kill The Threats On Sight

You’re sitting on the couch, snacks flying, watching your smart fridge wage war against a botnet in Eastern Europe. You think your firewall logs are a spectator sport?! You think the alerts are just gonna politely wait for you to finish your episode before burning your network to the ground?! Wake up, chief! 

We spent the last five chapters turning your crib into Fort Knox. We did the recon. We sliced the VLANs. We locked the doors. We choked the flow. We put Suricata on the streets to sniff out the rats. But if you’re just *watching* the logs like it’s a Netflix drama, you’re highkey delusional. 

You see a threat, you gotta drop it. No negotiations. No timeouts. No mercy. Welcome to the endgame. This is where we automate the execution.

## Core Carnage (Rip Apart the Essentials)

Manual response is for losers. If a hacker gets a foothold while you’re sleeping, you wake up robbed. Zero trust isn’t just about checking IDs at the door—it’s about blowing the bridge the second someone looks at you funny. We need auto-strikes. We need kill switches. 

**1. Automated IP Bans (The Boomstick)**
Some slacker script kiddie is hitting your firewall from the same IP, trying brute-force passwords on your exposed services over and over. Bang. You don’t wait for attempt number fifty. You set the tripwire. Three strikes? IP banned. Dropped. Blackholed. We use tools like `fail2ban` or your firewall’s native response actions to read the logs in real-time and swing the ban hammer automatically.

**2. Threat Intel Feeds (The Hit List)**
You ain't gotta do all the snitching yourself. There are entire intelligence agencies and security firms tracking the bad guys. We subscribe to threat intelligence feeds—lists of known malicious IPs, domains, and C2 (Command and Control) servers. Your firewall pulls this list every hour. If traffic hits your network from one of those IPs? Dead on arrival. Auto-blocked before the packet even finishes knocking.

**3. Immediate VLAN Isolation (The Quarantine)**
Your smart TV starts acting funky. Suddenly it’s scanning port 22 on your laptop. It’s infected. It’s patient zero. Do you let it roam free on the IoT VLAN? Hell no. You trap it. We set up triggers so when Suricata detects infection signs—lateral movement, weird C2 traffic, malware signatures—it instantly shoves that device into a quarantine VLAN. A digital solitary confinement cell. No internet. No LAN access. Just a brick wall until you manually clear it.

**4. Continuous Vulnerability Scans (The Sweep)**
The IoT market moves fast, and security is usually an afterthought. Your router gets a firmware update. Your smart bulb has a new zero-day. You gotta sweep your own house constantly. We schedule automated vulnerability scanners—like OpenVAS or Nessus—to crawl your network weekly. It exposes new weaknesses before the bad guys do. You find the hole, you patch it. 

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Threat Actor Hits Firewall] --> B{Firewall Logs Attack}
    B --> C[Fail2ban Reads Log]
    C --> D[Auto-Ban IP Address]
    
    E[IoT Device Gets Infected] --> F[Suricata Detects C2 Traffic]
    F --> G[Trigger Webhook/API]
    G --> H[Move Device to Quarantine VLAN]
    
    I[Threat Intel Feed Updates] --> J[Firewall Pulls New Blocklist]
    J --> K[Auto-Drop Known Malicious IPs]
    
    L[Scheduled Vulnerability Scan] --> M[Scanner Crawls Network]
    M --> N[Alert on New Weaknesses]
```

## Street Smarts (How to Wield This in the Wild)

Alright, dreamer. Time to get your hands dirty. We’re loading the weapons. Grab that OPNsense box we set up last time. We’re turning it into a fully automated hitman.

**Step 1: The Boomstick (Automated IP Bans with fail2ban)**

You got SSH exposed? A web server? Doesn't matter. If it logs failed logins, we ban the IP. 

1. Spin up a lightweight Linux box or container on your network. 
2. Install `fail2ban`. `sudo apt install fail2ban` on Debian/Ubuntu. Simple.
3. Configure the jail. You need to tell it what log to watch and what service to protect. Edit `/etc/fail2ban/jail.local`.
4. Set `maxretry = 3`. Set `bantime = 3600`. Or `bantime = -1` if you want to ban them forever. Your call, executioner.
5. Now, here’s the needle mover: You need `fail2ban` to talk to OPNsense. We use the `action` config to trigger an API call to your firewall. When `fail2ban` sees three failed logins, it hits the OPNsense API and drops the IP into an alias blocklist. 

> 💡 **Pro Tip:** Don't reinvent the wheel. Look up the `fail2ban-opnsense` action scripts on GitHub. They do the heavy lifting for you. You just plug in your API key and secret.

**Step 2: The Hit List (Threat Intel Feeds on OPNsense)**

You don't need to hunt every rat manually. Let the pros do it.

1. Go to `Firewall -> Aliases` in OPNsense.
2. Create a new alias. Name it `ThreatIntel_Blocked`.
3. Set the type to `URL Table`.
4. Grab a free feed. AlienVault OTX, Emerging Threats, or Spamhaus. Drop the URL in the box.
5. Set the refresh frequency to 1 hour. 
6. Go to `Firewall -> Rules -> LAN`. Create a block rule. Source: `ThreatIntel_Blocked`. Destination: `any`. 
7. Hit apply. Boom. Your firewall is now auto-updating its kill list every hour. Any traffic from a known bad IP gets instantly vaporized.

> ⚠️ **Common Mistake:** Pulling too many feeds and melting your firewall's RAM. Start with one or two highly reputable feeds. Don't try to download the entire internet's blocklist on a Raspberry Pi.

**Step 3: The Quarantine (Auto VLAN Isolation)**

This is the big leagues. This is where zero trust meets brute force. Your smart fridge starts spitting out Mirai worm traffic. We gotta trap it.

1. First, create a "Quarantine" VLAN in OPNsense. No internet access. No LAN routing. Just a dead zone.
2. Go back to `Services -> Intrusion Detection -> Administration` (where we left Suricata last chapter).
3. You need to configure Suricata's `eve.json` log output. This is the real-time stream of alerts.
4. We use a plugin or a simple Python script to tail the `eve.json` file. 
5. When the script sees a specific alert—say, a Mirai C2 callback—it triggers an OPNsense API call.
6. The API call changes the MAC address of the infected device from the `IoT_VLAN` alias to the `Quarantine_VLAN` alias.
7. The device is instantly cut off. No reboot required. The threat is neutralized while you're at work.

> 🎯 **Key Insight:** Automation is only as smart as your rules. If you quarantine a device because of a false positive, your wife loses Netflix, and you lose sleep. Tune your Suricata rules strictly for high-confidence malware callbacks before you wire them to the kill switch.

**Step 4: The Sweep (Continuous Vulnerability Scanning)**

You built the walls. Now check for cracks. Weekly.

1. Deploy OpenVAS (Greenbone) on a dedicated machine. It’s heavy, but it’s the industry standard.
2. Log into the web interface. Go to `Configuration -> Targets`.
3. Add your subnets. `192.168.10.0/24` for IoT. `192.168.20.0/24` for personal. 
4. Go to `Scans -> Schedules`. Set it to run every Sunday at 3 AM. 
5. Create a new scan task. Select your targets. Select your schedule. Hit start.
6. OpenVAS will crawl every device, checking for default passwords, outdated firmware, and open ports. 
7. Check the reports Monday morning. Fix what's broken. Patch what's old. 

> ☕ **Real Talk:** Vulnerability scans will show you ugly truths. You’ll find out your expensive smart doorbell is running a 5-year-old version of Linux with a root password of `admin`. That's the IoT game, bro. The scan gives you the map. It's up to you to fix the leaks or toss the device.

You wanted zero trust? This is the final boss. You’re not just defending the crib anymore. You’re actively hunting anything that steps out of line. The network polices itself. The threats get dropped on sight. 

Excuses? Cute. But winners bleed sweat, not stories. Your move, dreamer. You made it through the whole path. Now go build the kill switch.
